gpt_review.biasedText: China has a chokehold on the minerals which are used in the making of electric cars, electronics and military weapons.
gpt_review.reason: The phrase “chokehold” is a vivid, negative metaphor that implies coercive control. It appears in the author’s voice without attribution; while China’s dominance is later quantified, this wording adds subjective emphasis.

gpt_review.biasedText: These curbs have dealt a major blow to the US, whose industries are heavily dependent on imports of the precious metal.
gpt_review.reason: “Dealt a major blow” is an assertive characterization of impact and not attributed to a source. It goes beyond the data provided and frames the effect in dramatic terms.

gpt_review.biasedText: It is the reason why Beijing's export curbs have the ability to hit the US hard.
gpt_review.reason: “Hit the US hard” is emotive shorthand rather than neutral description, and is not tied to a specific quantified estimate in that sentence.

gpt_review.biasedText: China has a near monopoly on extracting rare earths
gpt_review.reason: “Near monopoly” is a strong descriptor. The article cites 61% of production and 92% of processing; the term arguably overstates extraction dominance and injects evaluative framing.

gpt_review.biasedText: This is largely believed to be part of why US president Donald Trump is so keen to sign a minerals deal with Ukraine - it wants to reduce dependency on China.
gpt_review.reason: “So keen to sign” imputes eagerness and uses vague attribution (“largely believed”) rather than sourcing, reflecting interpretive tone.

gpt_review.biasedText: Another place Trump has had his eye on is Greenland which is endowed with the eighth largest reserves of rare earth elements.
gpt_review.reason: “Had his eye on” is colloquial and interpretive, suggesting personal intent rather than stating concrete, sourced actions.

gpt_review.biasedText: the adversarial tone Trump has struck with them means the US could be left with very few alternative suppliers.
gpt_review.reason: Asserts both a characterization (“adversarial tone”) and causal outcome without explicit sourcing in that sentence, making it an interpretive claim.
